ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Mili Juli Sabzi; closest match is the generic mixed vegetable dish class FOODON_03301710.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (mixed vegetables, cooked) is the closest defensible category for this spiced North Indian mixed vegetable preparation. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for Mili Juli Sabzi.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mili Juli Sabzi is approximately 200 g, typically served as a side dish alongside roti, naan, or rice in a North Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Vegetable Curry (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for mixed vegetable curry (sabzi) prepared with common vegetables, tomatoes, onions, oil, and spices. Values are adapted to represent a typical restaurant-style Mili Juli Sabzi. Specific vegetable composition varies by season and restaurant; values represent a central estimate for a mixed vegetable preparation with oil and spices consistent with the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Vegetable Dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked mixed vegetables with spices
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mili Juli Sabzi. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous cooked mixed vegetable dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Non-starchy vegetables have inherently low GI values (typically 30–55). The addition of tomatoes, onions, and spices does not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 14.4) / 100 ≈ 6.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spices
hypertension.guidance: Mili Juli Sabzi contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage cumulative daily sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from non-starchy vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 45 and 2.4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Mili Juli Sabzi produces a modest postprandial glucose response and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal with controlled carbohydrate portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and antioxidant-rich vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mili Juli Sabzi is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and provides dietary fibre and antioxidant vitamins from seasonal vegetables, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should ensure oil quantity is minimised during preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mili Juli Sabzi (Hindi: मिली जुली सब्ज़ी, meaning 'mixed mixed vegetables') is a classic North Indian vegetable dish consisting of a medley of seasonal fresh vegetables sautéed or stir-fried with freshly ground spices, tomatoes, onions, and green chili. The name literally translates to 'mixed and combined vegetables' in Hindi. Common vegetables used include cauliflower, peas, carrots, beans, potatoes, capsicum, and spinach, though the exact composition varies by season and restaurant. The dish is cooked in oil with a base of onions and tomatoes, seasoned with cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and green chili, resulting in a mildly spiced, savoury preparation with a soft texture and vibrant colour. In Singapore, Mili Juli Sabzi is served at North Indian restaurants such as Curry Village, Bawarchi, and Aryana, typically as a vegetarian side dish accompanying roti, naan, paratha, or steamed rice. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI dish providing approximately 72 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful dietary fibre (2.4 g), vitamin C (18 mg), vitamin A (55 mcg RAE), and iron (1.1 mg). It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. The low saturated fat content and high vegetable diversity make it a clinically favourable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, provided sodium from added salt is moderated.
common_user_queries: Is Mili Juli Sabzi good for diabetics?
What vegetables are in Mili Juli Sabzi?
North Indian mixed vegetable dish Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Mili Juli Sabzi exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore databases. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry entry, which closely matches the described preparation method and ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked mixed vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium because the exact vegetable composition varies by restaurant and season.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) mixed vegetable curry entry. Exact vegetable composition varies by restaurant and season; values represent a central estimate. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero is consistent with the vegan composition of the dish. Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN mixed vegetable curry data. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN source. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked mixed non-starchy vegetable dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore including Curry Village (Boat Quay), Bawarchi (South Bridge Road), and Aryana (South Bridge Road). Price range SGD 14.90–15.90 per restaurant portion. Source: field survey data (used for restaurant and price metadata only; not used as nutritional evidence).
